free_text: 88Sr(p,n)88Y Reaction in the Proton Energy Range
from 4.4 to 8.0 MeV
The neutron detector was a 4pi counter
consisting of a 24-in. cube of polyethylene with four
3He neutron counters inserted radially toward the
center of the cube where target was placed.
A 3-in. diameter beam channel passes through the
center of the polyethylene cube so that the beam may
be stopped outside the cube. Proper radial
positioning of the counters allows approximately flat
energy response, and angular spacing of counters
allows angular variations of the yield to be
averaged. The polyethylene cube was used with four
detectors located at 56, 80, 100, and 124 degrees
with respect to the incident proton beam. The numbers
of counts in the 56 and 124 degrees detectors and in
the 80 and 100 degrees detectors, respectively, were
added and recorded. All of the counters were placed
at the same depth in the polyethylene as measured
from the outer boundary of the cylindrical cavity.
This procedure averages the counting rates in the
forward and backward hemispheres and gives a measure
of the deviation from isotropy of the neutron flux in
the cube. A measure of the neutron energy dependence
of the efficiency of the polyethylene-cube counter
was obtained by comparison of the excitation function
for 88Sr(p,n) 88Y observed with the cube to that
obtained with a long counter placed at a distance of
20 cm from the target at an angle of 90 degree to the
neutron beam. The background neutron production was
measured by substituting a carbon foil for the
carbon-backed target and measuring the neutron yield
for the same collected charge of the proton beam.

Fig.1.
Later experiment with high resolution
The relative cross sections for 88Sr(p,n)88Y
reaction are estimated on the comparison of the
24-in. cube with the long-counter measurements. The
absolute cross section was determined by
normalization to the cross section measured near the
5.05-MeV resonance and reported in the monitor, Ref.
of G.C.Dutt and R.Schrils.
The relative cross sections in Fig. 1 are
estimated to be accurate within +-8% based on the
comparison of the 24-in. cube with the long-counter
measurements. The absolute cross section (monitor
error) was estimated to be  accurate to +-20%.
ERR-T -> DATA-ERR; STATUS updated
Table II, page 2233.
Fits are obtained to both the (p, n) and
elastic scattering data of Cosman. The enhancement
factor used in the analysis of the (p,n) data is
different from the one used in previous analysis of
Robson. The following assumptions are made: (1) The
analog state is dominated by a single proton entrance
channel p but the reduced widths of the analog level
for other proton exit channels are not assumed to be
zero; (2) the background R matrix corresponding to
the T less states is assumed to be diagonal, and,
therefore, all direct-reaction contributions to the
(p,n) cross section are zero.
